learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of dispute resolution theory from different disciplinary and cultural perspectives.
Reflect critically on the theoretical assumptions underlying dispute resolution practice and how those assumptions shape process design, practitioner conduct, and skills training.
Explain the nature, advantages and disadvantages of various dispute resolution processes, from both a theoretical and a practical perspective.
Analyse practical, legal and ethical issues that arise in dispute resolution.
Critically evaluate the selection of an appropriate type of dispute resolution process according to the circumstances of a particular case.

assessments.task_description:
There will be 4 mini quizzes based on the allocated readings and the material covered in seminars. Each quiz will be online, and will be multiple choice or true/false.

The mini quizzes collectively contribute 10% of the final grade. Students are required to complete at least three of the four quizzes to be eligible for the full 10% available for this assessment task. Each completed quiz is worth 3.33% of the final grade. Where a student completes all four quizzes, only their best three quiz results will be counted towards the final mark for this assessment.

The mini quiz must be submitted electronically during class. You must be in the class seminar to undertake this mini-quiz
assessments.submission_guidelines: Instructions will be provided in class. You will need a computer connected to the Internet in class to do the assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Group presentation 40% (10 minutes plus approximately 5 minutes Q&A)

This is a team-based group presentation and will be discussed in the first seminar. In Seminar Weekend One, you will form a small group (4-5 students) to work together on the presentation. You may choose your own group members. 

Your task is to develop a proposal for a dispute resolution process to facilitate resolution of a conflict scenario. Different conflict scenarios will be made available on Learn.UQ (Blackboard). Your team has to choose one of the scenarios to work on and develop a proposal for how to facilitate dispute resolution for that scenario. Please note that not all possible DR processes that you may choose will have been discussed when this presentation is due. This means that you have to engage in independent reading for subsequent modules to be able to choose the appropriate DR process. You are welcome to contact the Course Coordinator with any questions you have and to discuss your approach.

You need to prepare a 10-minute team presentation to convince the stakeholder that your approach is appropriate and will assist in constructive engagement with the conflict. Explain why the suggested process is suitable for the particular conflict, what its advantages and disadvantages are and how you are going to deal with potential challenges and impasses. Identify and critically evaluated the theoretical assumptions underpinning your recommended process. Present a well-rounded proposal, which acknowledges weaknesses and challenges but also makes a persuasive argument based on the merits of the process chosen and its underlying theories.

Innovation and engaging interactive presentations are encouraged. Following your presentation, there will be 5 minutes of Q&A. All presentations will be conducted on Zoom. 


Due date

In class, you will be shown how to book a session for your group presentation, to be delivered online via Zoom. Session times will be available for week 7 (11-13 September) or week 8 (18-20 September). Exact dates and times for presentations TBC, depending on student enrolment numbers.


Format

Each oral presentation session will be approximately 2 hours, to allow multiple groups to present in one session. All group members must be present for the session. Your group will be presenting to the course coordinator and a small group of fellow students. The presentation will be recorded.


Group input

All group members must contribute to selecting, developing, researching, and presenting the topic. Every group member will receive the same mark, unless a case is made otherwise. 

Resources to support your group work will be provided on the course Blackboard and discussed in class.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Your task is to write a 3,000 word academic research essay on a current issue in dispute resolution. You will have a choice of topics, which will be released on Blackboard. 

The paper must be based on current and relevant scholarly (and practitioner if appropriate) literature, include a clear hypothesis or research problem and present a balanced argument. This means that you are required to read and reference literature which supports and literature which criticises your hypothesis. You are also required to reference any thought which is not your own or is considered general knowledge.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The assignment must be submitted electronically via the assessment submission link on the course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Seminars

Seminar topics are:

Seminar 1 – Foundations: Understanding Conflict and Dispute Resolution

Module 1: Understanding conflict: Course overview; Disciplinary and theoretical frameworks for understanding conflict and dispute resolution

Module 2: Development of dispute resolution: The development of dispute resolution in Australia; Values and goals of dispute resolution; Facilitative, advisory and determinative processes; Interest-based, rights-based and power-based models as an analytical framework


Seminar 2 - Core Dispute Resolution Processes: Negotiation, Mediation and Advisory Processes

Module 3: Negotiation: Negotiation as the foundation for constructive conflict resolution; Distributive and integrative approaches to negotiation; Analytical frameworks for negotiation; The theory and practice of principled negotiation

Module 4: Mediation and conciliation: Mediation - concepts, definitions, and theoretical approaches; Facilitative, evaluative and transformative models compared; Conciliation and other advisory processes


Seminar 3 – Determinative Processes and the Legal and Ethical Dimensions of Dispute Resolution

Module 5: Determinative processes and the legal architecture of dispute resolution: Arbitration - history, domestic and international arbitration, theoretical underpinnings, and critiques; Legal issues arising in DR: Confidentiality, practitioner conduct, liability and enforceability of outcomes; Integration of DR into justice systems – dispute systems design theory and process selection

Module 6: Power, ethics and the limits of neutrality: Party autonomy, voluntariness, and the critique of mandatory DR; Power, neutrality, and impartiality -theoretical frameworks and practitioner implications; Ethics, standards of practice and practitioner accountability 


Seminar 4 – Culture, Context and Emerging Directions

Module 7: The role of culture in dispute resolution: Culture and conflict – understanding the cultural underpinnings of dispute resolution; Critical analysis of Eurocentric approaches; First Nations perspectives on conflict and resolution; Restorative practices

Module 8: Frontiers of dispute resolution: Technology, innovation and access to justice, including ODR and AI in dispute resolution; Complex, public and international conflict – value disputes, facilitating public dialogue, international and transitional justice; Critical synthesis and reflection on dispute resolution as an evolving field.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
